concepts.title: Methods for finding principal values
concepts.conceptBody:
Method for finding principal value 

Step I. Mark the quadrant in which the angle may lie.

Step II. Mark the direction to be taken. Select anticlockwise direction for 1st and 2nd

quadrants and select clockwise direction for 3rd and 4th quadrants.

Step III. Find the angles in the first circle.

Step IV. Select the numerically smallest angle. i.e., in the first rotation.

In case, two values, one with positive sign and the other with negative sign,

qualify for principal value, we conventionally select the angle with positive sign

as principal value.

concepts.title: Differentiation of inverse trigonometric functions
concepts.conceptBody:
Differentiation and integration of inverse trigonometric functions 

To differentiate the arc tangent function, we imitate the method we used to differentiate

the logarithm function. Namely, if $y=\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\left( \text{x} \right)$, then $\text{tan}\left( \text{y} \right)=\text{x}$, so

$\frac{d}{dx}\text{tan}\left( y \right)=\frac{d}{dx}x\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ Hence }\!\!~\!\!\text{ se}{{\text{c}}^{2}}\left( y \right)\frac{dy}{dx}=1$

from which it follows that $\frac{dy}{dx}=\frac{1}{\text{se}{{\text{c}}^{2}}\left( y \right)}$

Now, $\text{se}{{\text{c}}^{2}}\left( y \right)=1+\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{2}}\left( y \right)=1+{{x}^{2}}$

so we have $\frac{dy}{dx}=\frac{1}{1+{{x}^{2}}}$

Hence we have demonstrated the following proposition.

Proposition $:\frac{\text{d}}{\text{dx}}\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\left( \text{x} \right)=\frac{1}{1+{{\text{x}}^{2}}}$

As a consequence of the proposition, we have $\int \frac{1}{1+{{x}^{2}}}dx=\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\left( x \right)+c$

$1+{{\text{x}}^{2}}$ is an irreducible polynomial. We will see more example of this type in the

following examples.

Using the chain rule, we have $\frac{d}{dx}\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\left( 4{{x}^{2}} \right)=\frac{8x}{1+16{{x}^{4}}}$

concepts.title: Integration of inverse trigonometric functions
concepts.conceptBody:
Integration of inverse trigonometric functions

Evaluating $\int \text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\left( x \right)dx$ is similar to evaluating $\int \text{log}\left( x \right)dx$. 

That is, we will use

integration by parts with

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \text{u}=\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\left( \text{x} \right) & \text{v}=\text{x} \\ \text{du}=\frac{1}{1+{{\text{x}}^{2}}}\text{dx} & \text{dv}=\text{dx} \\ \end{array}$

Then, $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\int \text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}xdx=x\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}x-\int \frac{x}{1+{{x}^{2}}}dx$

Using the substitution,

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & u=1+{{x}^{2}} \\ {} & du=2xdx \\ \end{array}$

we have, $\frac{1}{2}du=xdx$, from which it follows that

$\int ~\frac{\text{x}}{1+{{\text{x}}^{2}}}\text{dx}=\frac{1}{2}\int ~\frac{1}{\text{u}}\text{du}=\frac{1}{2}\text{log}\left| \text{u} \right|+\text{c}=\frac{1}{2}\text{log}\left( 1+{{\text{x}}^{2}} \right)+\text{c}$

Thus, $\int \text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\left( \text{x} \right)\text{dx}=\text{xta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\left( \text{x} \right)-\frac{1}{2}\text{log}\left( 1+{{\text{x}}^{2}} \right)+\text{c}$
